docs.title: The AI Coder's Journey: From Zero to Chatbot
docs.text:
Student: Future Architect

Equipment: Mac Computer

Goal: Build a real AI Chatbot

Welcome, future coder! You are about to begin an epic quest. Like any great adventure game, you will start with nothing — no special tools, no secret knowledge — and level up one step at a time. By the end of this journey, you will have built your own AI-powered chatbot. Let's go!

docs.title: What You Will Need
docs.text:
Before you start, make sure you have:

  • [ ] A Mac computer (MacBook Air, MacBook Pro, iMac — any Mac works)
  • [ ] An internet connection (we will download tools along the way)
  • [ ] About 30 minutes for the initial setup (Level 0)
  • [ ] A parent or adult nearby — some steps require your computer's password

docs.title: What you do NOT need
docs.text:
• No coding experience — we start from absolute zero
  • No prior Terminal or command-line knowledge
  • No special software already installed — we will install everything together

docs.title: How This Guide Works
docs.text:
This guide is organized like a video game:

  • Level 0 is the setup — you will equip your utility belt with all the tools you need.
  • Chapters 1–7 are the journey — each chapter is a new level where you unlock new skills.
  • Every step is hands-on — you will type real commands and see real results.
  • Claude Code is your AI companion — it lives in your Terminal and helps you every step of the way. You will install it in Level 0 and use it throughout every chapter.

docs.title: Jargon Buster
docs.text:
You will see these words a lot. Here is what they mean before you dive in:

Word What It Means Terminal The cockpit of your computer — a text window where you type commands instead of clicking icons. Command A text instruction you type into Terminal to tell your computer what to do. Install Download and set up a new tool on your computer so it is ready to use. Homebrew The app store for coders — a tool that installs other tools using simple commands. VS Code A free text editor made for writing code — think of it as Microsoft Word, but for programming. Node.js A tool that lets JavaScript (a programming language) run on your computer, not just in a browser. Claude Code Your AI coding companion — it lives inside Terminal and answers questions, writes code, and helps you debug. Folder / Directory A container for files on your computer. "Folder" and "directory" mean the same thing. Script A file full of commands that your computer can run all at once — like a recipe. Python A beginner-friendly programming language used for AI, web servers, games, and more.

docs.title: Level 0 — The Setup (Equipping the Utility Belt)
docs.text:
Before we write code, we need tools. This level walks you through installing everything, one step at a time. Do not skip ahead — each tool depends on the one before it.

By the end of Level 0, you will have: Homebrew, Node.js, Claude Code, VS Code, Python, and Git — all ready to go.

docs.title: Mission 0.1: Open Terminal
docs.text:
Terminal is the cockpit of your computer. Instead of clicking icons, you type text commands. Every developer uses it.

How to find it:

  1. Press Command + Space to open Spotlight (a search bar appears at the top of your screen).
  2. Type Terminal and press Enter.
  3. A window will appear with text and a blinking cursor.

You should see something like this:

What you are looking at:

  • The text before the cursor (usually ending in % or $) is called the prompt — it means Terminal is ready and waiting for your command.
  • You type commands after the prompt and press Enter to run them.
  • Do not worry if the colors or text look a little different on your Mac — that is normal.

You just opened the cockpit. Let's install your first tool!

docs.title: Mission 0.2: Install Homebrew (The App Store for Coders)
docs.text:
Homebrew is a tool that lets you install other developer tools with a simple command. Think of it as the app store for coders — but instead of clicking "Get," you type a command.

Parent assistance recommended for this step — you may need to enter your Mac's password.

  1. Copy and paste this entire command into Terminal, then press Enter:
/bin/bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Homebrew/install/HEAD/install.sh)"

  1. Follow the instructions on screen. When it asks for your password, type your Mac login password (the screen will not show any characters as you type — that is normal and a security feature).

  2. Important for Apple Silicon Macs (M1, M2, M3, M4): After Homebrew finishes installing, it will show you two extra commands you need to run to add Homebrew to your PATH. They will look something like this:

echo >> ~/.zprofile
echo 'eval "$(/opt/homebrew/bin/brew shellenv)"' >> ~/.zprofile
eval "$(/opt/homebrew/bin/brew shellenv)"

Copy and paste the exact commands that Homebrew shows you — they may be slightly different from the example above.

  1. Verify Homebrew is installed by typing:
brew --version

You should see a version number like Homebrew 4.x.x. If you see command not found: brew, go back and run the PATH commands from step 3.

Tool unlocked: Homebrew! Now you can install anything.

docs.title: Mission 0.3: Install Node.js
docs.text:
Node.js lets JavaScript (a programming language) run directly on your computer. We need it right now because Claude Code requires Node.js to work.

  1. In Terminal, type:
brew install node

  1. Wait for it to finish (you will see progress text scrolling by — that is normal).

  2. Verify it worked:

node -v

You should see a version number like v22.x.x.

  1. Also check that npm (Node's package manager) is installed:
npm -v

You should see a version number like 10.x.x.

Tool unlocked: Node.js! Now we can install Claude Code.

docs.title: Mission 0.4: Install Claude Code (Your AI Companion)
docs.text:
This is the exciting one! Claude Code is your AI companion for the entire journey. It lives right inside your Terminal, answers your questions, explains code, writes code, and helps you debug when things go wrong. You will use it in every chapter from here on out.

  1. Install Claude Code using npm (the package manager that came with Node.js):
npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code

The -g means "install globally" — this makes Claude Code available from any folder on your computer.

  1. Start Claude Code for the first time:
claude
  1. Follow the login instructions that appear on screen. You will need to:
  2. Create an account or log in at console.anthropic.com

  3. Authorize Claude Code to connect to your account

  4. Once authenticated, try asking Claude your first question! Type this in Terminal:

claude "What is Terminal on a Mac? Explain it like I am a beginner."

Watch as Claude gives you a friendly, helpful answer right inside your Terminal!

  1. Try one more — ask Claude about what you just installed:
claude "What is Homebrew and why do coders use it?"

Congratulations! You now have an AI companion by your side. Whenever you are stuck, confused, or curious, you can ask Claude. It is like having a patient, friendly tutor who never gets tired of your questions.

docs.title: Mission 0.5: Install VS Code (Your Code Editor)
docs.text:
VS Code (Visual Studio Code) is a free text editor designed for writing code. Think of it as Microsoft Word, but built for programmers.

  1. In Terminal, type:
brew install --cask visual-studio-code

The --cask flag tells Homebrew this is a full desktop application (with a window and menus), not a small command-line tool.

  1. Open VS Code:
  2. Press Command + Space, type Visual Studio Code, and press Enter.
  3. Or find it in your Applications folder.

  1. Bonus: You can open VS Code from Terminal by typing:
code .

The . means "open the current folder." This is a shortcut you will use constantly.

If code . does not work, open VS Code, press Command + Shift + P, type "shell command," and click "Install 'code' command in PATH."

Tool unlocked: VS Code! You now have a professional code editor.

docs.title: Mission 0.6: Install Python
docs.text:
Python is a beginner-friendly programming language that you will use in several chapters (especially Chapters 3–5). It is the language behind most AI applications.

  1. In Terminal, type:
brew install python@3.12
  1. Verify it worked:
python3 --version

You should see something like Python 3.12.x.

Tool unlocked: Python! You are ready for Chapters 3–5.

docs.title: Mission 0.7: Install Git (The Time Machine for Code)
docs.text:
Git is a tool that tracks every change you make to your code — like a time machine that lets you go back to any previous version. You will use it in Chapter 6.

  1. In Terminal, type:
brew install git
  1. Verify it worked:
git --version

You should see something like git version 2.x.x.

Tool unlocked: Git! You now have a time machine for your code.

docs.title: Setup Complete!
docs.text:
Congratulations, adventurer! Your utility belt is fully loaded. You have installed every tool you need for the entire journey. Take a moment to celebrate — most professional developers use these exact same tools every day, and you just set them all up.

Here is everything you installed:

  • [x] Terminal — the cockpit of your computer
  • [x] Homebrew — the app store for coders
  • [x] Node.js — lets JavaScript run on your computer
  • [x] Claude Code — your AI coding companion
  • [x] VS Code — your code editor
  • [x] Python 3.12 — a programming language for AI and web servers
  • [x] Git — a time machine for your code

You are ready to begin Chapter 1. Let the quest begin!

docs.title: The Chapters
docs.text: Each chapter below is a new level. Work through them in order — skills from earlier chapters are used in later ones.

docs.title: 🖥️ Chapter 1 — Shell Commands 101: Becoming a Terminal Wizard
docs.text:
Now that Terminal is open, it is time to learn the commands that every developer uses daily. You will navigate folders, create files, move things around, and start feeling like a true Terminal wizard.

Skills you will unlock: - Navigate your file system with cd, ls, and pwd - Create, copy, move, and remove files and folders - Read files and search for text from the command line - Chain commands together like a pro

Start Chapter 1 →

Ask Claude: claude "What does the ls -la command do in Terminal?"

docs.title: ⚙️ Chapter 2 — Environment Variables, PATH, and Shell Scripts
docs.text:
Discover the hidden powers of the shell! You will learn about environment variables (secret settings your computer uses), the PATH (how your computer finds tools), and how to write your first shell script.

Skills you will unlock: - Understand what environment variables are and how to set them - Learn how PATH works and why it matters - Write and run your first shell script (greeter.sh) - Customize your Terminal environment

Start Chapter 2 →

Ask Claude: claude "Explain what the PATH environment variable does like I am 12 years old"

docs.title: 🗂️ Chapter 2.1 — Terminal, Finder, and VS Code: Three Views of the Same Files
docs.text:
Your Mac has three different ways to look at the same files — Finder (the icon view), Terminal (the text view), and VS Code (the code view). This chapter shows you how they all connect.

Skills you will unlock: - Understand that Finder, Terminal, and VS Code show the same files - Navigate between all three tools confidently - Open folders from Terminal in Finder or VS Code - Stop feeling lost when switching between tools

Start Chapter 2.1 →

Ask Claude: claude "How do I open the current Terminal folder in Finder on a Mac?"

docs.title: 🗑️ Chapter 2.2 — Deleting Files and Folders Safely in Terminal
docs.text:
Deleting files in Terminal is powerful — and permanent. This chapter teaches you how to delete safely so you never accidentally destroy something important.

Skills you will unlock: - Understand the difference between Trash and rm (permanent delete) - Delete files and folders safely from Terminal - Learn protective habits before running destructive commands - Recover from common deletion mistakes

Start Chapter 2.2 →

Ask Claude: claude "What is the difference between moving a file to Trash and using rm in Terminal?"

docs.title: 🐍 Chapter 3 — Introduction to Python Programming
docs.text:
Time to learn your first real programming language! Python is one of the easiest and most powerful languages in the world. You will write scripts, use variables, loops, functions, and even virtual environments.

Skills you will unlock: - Write and run Python scripts from Terminal - Use variables, loops, conditionals, and functions - Create and manage virtual environments - Install Python packages with pip

Start Chapter 3 →

Ask Claude: claude "Write a simple Python script that asks for my name and says hello"

docs.title: 🌐 Chapter 3.1 — Your First Web Server
docs.text:
Make your computer talk to the internet! You will build a web server using Python and Node.js — a real program that other computers can connect to.

Skills you will unlock: - Understand what a web server is and how it works - Build a simple server with Python (FastAPI) - Build a simple server with Node.js - Send and receive data between a browser and your server

Start Chapter 3.1 →

Ask Claude: claude "What is a web server and why would I want to build one?"

docs.title: 🌐 Chapter 3.2 — Introduction to HTML & JavaScript
docs.text:
Build your first web pages! You will learn HTML (the structure of web pages) and JavaScript (the language that makes them interactive), then connect them to your servers.

Skills you will unlock: - Write HTML to structure a web page - Use JavaScript to make pages interactive - Connect a web page to a backend server - Understand how frontend and backend work together

Start Chapter 3.2 →

Ask Claude: claude "Explain HTML and JavaScript like I am building with LEGO bricks"

docs.title: 🗄️ Chapter 4 — Databases & SQLite: Teaching Your Apps to Remember
docs.text:
So far, everything you have built forgets its data when you close it. Databases fix that — they save information permanently. You will learn SQL and build apps that remember.

Skills you will unlock: - Understand what a database is and why it matters - Create tables, insert data, and query with SQL - Use SQLite from Python - Build an app that saves and retrieves data

Start Chapter 4 →

Ask Claude: claude "What is SQL and how is it different from Python?"

docs.title: 🤖 Chapter 5 — Your First AI App: Teaching Computers to Think
docs.text:
This is the big one! You will get an AI API key, call an AI model, and build your own mini chatbot. By the end, you will have an AI-powered app that actually thinks.

Skills you will unlock: - Get and use an AI API key - Send prompts to an AI model and receive responses - Build a tiny AI chatbot - Understand prompts, tokens, and how AI models work

Start Chapter 5 →

Ask Claude: claude "What is an API key and why do I need one to use AI?"

docs.title: 🗂️ Chapter 6 — Introduction to Git & GitHub
docs.text:
Remember that time machine we installed? Now you learn how to use it. Git saves every version of your code, and GitHub stores it safely online — like iCloud for code, but more powerful.

Skills you will unlock: - Initialize a Git repository - Stage, commit, and push your code - Create a GitHub account and repository - Clone repositories and collaborate with others

Start Chapter 6 →

Ask Claude: claude "Explain git commit like I am saving a video game"

docs.title: 📝 Chapter 6.1 — README.md & Markdown Basics
docs.text:
Every great project needs great documentation. You will learn Markdown (the language used to write README files) and create professional-looking project pages on GitHub.

Skills you will unlock: - Write Markdown for headers, lists, links, and code blocks - Create a README.md that explains your project - Format text for GitHub and other platforms - Document your code like a pro

Start Chapter 6.1 →

Ask Claude: claude "Show me the most common Markdown formatting examples"

docs.title: 🌐 Chapter 7 — Introduction to Networking Tools
docs.text:
Learn how computers talk to each other across the internet. You will use tools like curl, ping, and dig to explore how data travels around the world.

Skills you will unlock: - Understand IP addresses, ports, and DNS - Use curl to make HTTP requests from Terminal - Use ping and dig to explore the network - Debug network problems like a real sysadmin

Start Chapter 7 →

Ask Claude: claude "How does my computer know where google.com is on the internet?"

docs.title: Using Claude Code Throughout Your Journey
docs.text: Claude Code is not just for setup — it is your companion for every chapter. Here are four ways to use it as you learn:

docs.title: 1. Explain something you do not understand
docs.text: claude "What does this Python code do? for i in range(5): print(i)"

docs.title: 2. Debug an error
docs.text:
When you see a scary error message, copy it and ask Claude:

claude "I got this error: ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'flask'. What does it mean and how do I fix it?"

docs.title: 3. Write code for you
docs.text: claude "Write a Python script that counts from 1 to 100 and prints 'fizz' for multiples of 3"

docs.title: 4. Review your code
docs.text:
claude "Look at my file server.py and tell me if there are any bugs"

Tip: Make sure you are inside your project folder (use cd to navigate there) before asking Claude questions about your files. Claude Code can see and edit files in your current directory.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
Stuck? Here are fixes for the most common problems:

"command not found: brew" Homebrew is not in your PATH. If you have an Apple Silicon Mac (M1/M2/M3/M4), run the PATH commands that Homebrew showed during installation. See Mission 0.2, step 3.

"command not found: code" VS Code's shell command is not set up. Open VS Code, press Command + Shift + P, type "shell command," and click "Install 'code' command in PATH."

"command not found: claude" Node.js may not be installed, or npm's global bin directory is not in your PATH. Run node -v to check. If Node.js is not found, go back to Mission 0.3. If Node.js works but claude is not found, try closing and reopening Terminal.

"Permission denied" Some commands need extra permission. Try adding sudo before the command (e.g., sudo brew install git). You will need to enter your Mac password. Only use sudo when a command specifically tells you permission is denied.

"Virtual environment is gone" or "No module named ..." If you closed Terminal and your Python packages stopped working, you need to reactivate your virtual environment. Navigate to your project folder and run:

source venv/bin/activate

When in doubt, ask Claude! Type claude "I got this error: <paste your error here>" and Claude will help you fix it.

docs.title: What Comes Next
docs.text:
You made it through the entire guide — from opening Terminal for the very first time to building your own AI-powered application. That is an incredible achievement.

From here, the world is open. Build your own projects, explore new languages, and keep experimenting. And remember — Claude Code is always right there in your Terminal, ready to help whenever you need it.

Happy coding, adventurer!

docs.title: The AI Coder's Journey: From Zero to Chatbot
docs.text:
Student: Future Architect

Equipment: Mac Computer

Goal: Build a real AI Chatbot

docs.title: Level 0: The Setup (Equipping the Utility Belt)
docs.text: Before we write code, we need tools. We will use the Terminal, which is like the cockpit of your computer.

docs.title: Mission 0.1: Summon the Terminal
docs.text:
1. Press Command + Space to open Spotlight.
  2. Type "Terminal" and hit Enter.
  3. You should see a black or white window with text. This is where we command the computer.

docs.title: Mission 0.2: Install Homebrew (The App Store for Coders)
docs.text: Parent assistance recommended here. Copy and paste this magic spell into your Terminal and hit Enter: /bin/bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Homebrew/install/HEAD/install.sh)" (Follow the instructions on screen. You may need to enter your computer password).

docs.title: Mission 0.3: Install VS Code (Your text editor)
docs.text:
We need a place to write our code.

  1. In Terminal, type: brew install --cask visual-studio-code
  2. Open VS Code from your Applications folder.

docs.title: Level 1: Browser Magic (JavaScript)
docs.text: We don't need to install anything to speak the language of the web. It's already in your browser!

docs.title: Mission 1.1: The Inspector
docs.text:
1. Open Google Chrome or Safari.
  2. Right-click anywhere on a webpage and select Inspect.
  3. Click the tab that says Console.

docs.title: Mission 1.2: Your First Spell
docs.text:
In the Console, type these exactly and hit Enter:

alert("Warning: Genius at work!");

Did a box pop up? You just wrote JavaScript!

docs.title: Mission 1.3: Variables (The Box Concept)
docs.text: Computers have bad memories. We use Variables to help them remember things. Type this: let heroName \= "Code Wizard"; alert("Hello " + heroName);

docs.title: Mission 1.4: The Math Machine
docs.text: let apples \= 5; let oranges \= 10; alert("Total fruit: " + (apples + oranges));

docs.title: Level 2: The Serpent (Server-Side Python)
docs.text: JavaScript lives in the browser, but Python lives on the server (the computer's brain).

docs.title: Mission 2.1: Wake the Snake
docs.text: In your Terminal, install Python: brew install python Check if it's alive: python3 --version

docs.title: Mission 2.2: The First Script
docs.text:
1. Create a folder on your desktop named coding_projects.
  2. Open that folder in VS Code.
  3. Create a new file named hello.py.
  4. Type this inside: import time

print("Initiating launch sequence...") time.sleep(1) print("3...") time.sleep(1) print("2...") time.sleep(1) print("1...") print("Blast off! 🚀")

  1. Run it: Open Terminal in VS Code (Terminal -> New Terminal) and type: python3 hello.py

docs.title: Level 3: The AI Partner (Claude Code)
docs.text: Coding is harder alone. We will hire an AI assistant named Claude to live in our terminal and help us.

docs.title: Mission 3.1: Install Node.js & Claude Code
docs.text:
Claude Code needs a tool called Node.js.

  1. In Terminal: brew install node
  2. Now install Claude: npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code

docs.title: Mission 3.2: Authenticate
docs.text:
1. Type: claude login
  2. Follow the instructions to connect your account.

docs.title: Mission 3.3: Pair Programming
docs.text:
Let's ask Claude to explain our Python script. In the terminal, inside your project folder: claude "Explain what hello.py does in simple terms"

Now, let's ask Claude to improve it:

claude "Rewrite hello.py to count down from 10 and make it sound like a robot"

Notice how Claude edits your file for you? This is the future of coding.

docs.title: Level 4: The Time Machine (Git & GitHub)
docs.text: Imagine if you could save your game before fighting a boss. Git lets you save your code so you never lose it.

docs.title: Mission 4.1: Install Git
docs.text: brew install git

docs.title: Mission 4.2: The Time Capsule
docs.text:
1. Go to GitHub.com and create an account.
  2. Create a New Repository named my-first-bot.

  3. In your Terminal: git init git add . git commit -m "My first backup!"

  4. Follow the instructions on GitHub to "push" your code.

docs.title: Level 5: The Web Stack (HTML + FastAPI)
docs.text: Now we build a "Server" (Python) that talks to a "Web Page" (HTML/JS).

docs.title: Mission 5.1: Install FastAPI
docs.text: FastAPI helps Python talk to the internet. pip3 install fastapi uvicorn

docs.title: Mission 5.2: The Backend (server.py)
docs.text:
Create a file server.py. This is our API.

from fastapi import FastAPI from fastapi.middleware.cors import CORSMiddleware

app \= FastAPI()

# This allows our web page to talk to this server app.add_middleware( CORSMiddleware, allow_origins=["*"], allow_methods=["*"], allow_headers=["*"], )

@app.get("/") def home(): return {"message": "System Online"}

@app.get("/add") def add_numbers(a: int, b: int): return {"result": a + b}

Run it: uvicorn server:app --reload Go to http://127.0.0.1:8000 in your browser. You should see the message!

docs.title: Mission 5.3: The Frontend (index.html)
docs.text:
Create index.html. This is the user interface.

\<!DOCTYPE html> \<html> \<body> \<h1>Super Calculator\</h1> \<input type="number" id="num1" placeholder="First Number"> \<input type="number" id="num2" placeholder="Second Number"> \<button onclick="calculate()">Add\</button> \<h2 id="result">\</h2>

\<script\>  
    async function calculate() {  
        let n1 \= document.getElementById("num1").value;  
        let n2 \= document.getElementById("num2").value;

        // Talk to our Python Server  
        let response \= await fetch(\`http://127.0.0.1:8000/add?a=${n1}\&b=${n2}\`);  
        let data \= await response.json();

        document.getElementById("result").innerText \= "Result: " \+ data.result;  
    }  
\</script\>

\</body> \</html>

docs.title: 🖥️ Chapter 1 — Shell Commands 101: Becoming a Terminal Wizard
docs.text: Level: Beginner Theme: Talking to your computer like a pro Tools: MacBook Air, Terminal (zsh) Goal: Learn the core shell commands used by all developers.

docs.title: ⭐ Level 1 — “The Terminal is Your Superpower”
docs.text:
The Terminal lets you talk to your computer using text instead of clicking icons.

VS Code is used in this tutorial

You can install VS Code via the download or via brew

brew install --cask visual-studio-code

docs.title: Open Terminal
docs.text: Command-space and type terminal

docs.title: 🔍 Command #1 — pwd (Where am I?)
docs.text: Stands for: Print Working Directory Meaning: "Show me the exact folder I'm in."

docs.title: Try it:
docs.text:
pwd

You’ll see something like:

/Users/myname

This is your current location in the computer.

docs.title: 📁 Command #2 — ls (What’s here?)
docs.text: Meaning: "List all files and folders inside the current directory."

docs.title: Try it:
docs.text: ls

docs.title: Useful flags:
docs.text:
ls -l    # detailed list
ls -a    # show hidden files
ls -lh   # human readable sizes

docs.title: Create a new folder:
docs.text:
mkdir my_first_folder

Check it:

ls

You should now see my_first_folder.

docs.title: Enter a folder:
docs.text:
cd my_first_folder
ls

docs.title: Check where you are:
docs.text: pwd

docs.title: Go back one directory:
docs.text:
cd ..
ls

docs.title: Go to your home directory:
docs.text:
cd ~
ls

docs.title: Go to the system root:
docs.text:
cd /
ls

docs.title: Back to your home directory:
docs.text:
cd ~
ls

docs.title: Open Finder
docs.text:
open .

Your screen will look different than mine since I have a lot more files/folders and customizations

docs.title: Take a Screenshot
docs.text:
Command-Shift-4 then drag to lasso a region. Screenshots show up on the Desktop 

ls ~/Desktop/

docs.title: 📝 Command #5 — echo (Make the computer speak)
docs.text: cd ~/my_first_folder

docs.title: Print text:
docs.text: echo "Hello world"

docs.title: Save text into a file:
docs.text:
echo "This is my first file" > note.txt

View the file:

ls
cat note.txt

Open VS Code in the curent working directory:

code .

Delete the file:

rm note.txt

See that it is gone

ls

Create it again

echo "This is my first file" > note.txt

docs.title: Create, Read, Update, Delete - CRUD
docs.text: Create a file (touch or echo "stuff" > file.txt) Read a file (cat) Update a file (VS Code) Delete a file (rm)

docs.title: ♻️ Bonus Magic — The while Loop
docs.text:
A loop repeats commands until you stop it.

A basic shell script - some programming!

docs.title: Try this loop:
docs.text:
while true
do
  echo "I am learning shell"
  sleep 1
done

Stop or break with CTRL + C.

docs.title: Another loop:
docs.text:
while true
do
  cat note.txt
  sleep 1
done

docs.title: 🚀 Example: Countdown Script
docs.text:
count=5
while [ $count -gt 0 ]
do
  echo "Countdown: $count"
  count=$((count - 1))
  sleep 1
done
echo "Blast off"

docs.title: 🧪 Mini Project: Create Your First Shell Script
1. Create a file:
docs.text: touch hello.sh

docs.title: 2. Open it to edit:
docs.text:
code hello.sh

You can also use the built-in text editor for MacOS

open -e hello.sh

Paste:

echo "Welcome to my first script!"
echo "Today is a great day to code."

OR

docs.title: 3. Back in Terminal, make it executable:
docs.text: chmod +x hello.sh

docs.title: 4. Run it:
docs.text:
./hello.sh

You now have a reusable script, a program that can run commands again and again within the computer. This style scripting will work on Mac and Linux servers. The Linux servers are the ones running most of the Internet/Web sites as well as the backends for the mobile apps that you use every day.

docs.title: Quest 1: Folder Factory
docs.text:
Within your home directory, create a folder called lab, then inside it create:

project1
project2
project3
project4
project5

Create a file in project1 called my_file_in_project_1.txt

docs.title: Quest 2: Mega Loop
docs.text: Print your name 20 times using a while loop.

docs.title: Quest 3: Echo Art
docs.text:
Use echo to create ASCII art inside a file.

echo " /\\_/\\
( o.o )
 > ^ <" > cat.txt

This create a file called cat.txt

cat cat.txt

cat << "EOF" > robot.txt
 [::::]
 | 0 0|
 | -- |
 |____|
  /  \
 /____\
EOF

cat robot.txt

docs.title: Quest 4: Explorer Mode
docs.text:
Navigate to your Documents folder using only:

cd
cd ..
pwd
ls

docs.title: 🎓 End of Chapter 1
docs.text:
You now know the essential commands: - pwd - ls - mkdir - cd - echo - while - cat - Redirecting output (>) - Running scripts (./file.sh)

Next up: 👉 Chapter 2 — Environment Variables, PATH, and Shell Scripts

docs.title: ⚙️ Chapter 2 — Environment Variables, PATH, and Shell Scripts
docs.text: Level: Beginner Theme: Hidden powers of the shell Tools: MacBook Air, Terminal (zsh) Goal: Understand environment variables, PATH, and create real CLI tools.

docs.title: 🌱 What Are Environment Variables?
docs.text: Environment variables are computer memory slots that store: - usernames - API keys - system locations - configuration

docs.title: View all env variables:
docs.text: printenv

docs.title: View a single variable:
docs.text:
echo $HOME
echo $USER
echo $SHELL

docs.title: Temporary env variable (lasts until you close Terminal):
docs.text:
export FAVORITE_FRUIT="mango"

Check it:

echo $FAVORITE_FRUIT

docs.title: 🏠 Permanent Env Variables
docs.text: To make variables permanent, add them to .zshrc.

docs.title: Open your config file in Text Editor
docs.text:
open -e ~/.zshrc

Add:

export FAVORITE_FRUIT="mango"

Reload:

source ~/.zshrc

Note: The source command can also be used with another shell script to set env variables that you might want on a temporary basis.

Now the variable persists forever.

docs.title: 🚚 What is PATH?
docs.text:
PATH is a list of folders your computer searches when you type a command.

View it:

echo $PATH

Example output:

/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/bin:/opt/homebrew/bin

When you type:

python

Your computer looks in each folder until it finds the program.

docs.title: ➕ Add a Folder to Your PATH
1. Create a folder for custom commands:
docs.text: mkdir ~/bin

docs.title: 2. Add it to PATH:
docs.text:
Open config:

open -e ~/.zshrc

Add:

export PATH="$PATH:$HOME/bin"

Reload:

source ~/.zshrc

Now any script inside ~/bin can be run from anywhere!

docs.title: 1. Create a script:
docs.text:
mkdir myfolder
cd myfolder

code hello.sh

Paste:

#!/bin/zsh
echo "Hello, new developer!"

docs.title: 2. Make it executable:
docs.text: chmod +x hello.sh

docs.title: 3. Run it:
docs.text:
./hello.sh

🎉 You created a real command on your computer!

docs.title: 📜 Create More Shell Scripts
docs.text: Create a new file called greeter.sh

docs.title: greeter.sh
docs.text:
#!/bin/zsh
echo "What is your name?"
read NAME
echo "Hello $NAME, welcome to scripting!"

chmod +x greeter.sh

Run it:

./greeter.sh

docs.title: countdown.sh - Loop Example
docs.text:
#!/bin/zsh
echo "Countdown from 5..."
for i in {5..1}
do
  echo $i
  sleep 1
done
echo "Blast off! 🚀"

chmod +x countdown.sh

Run it:

./countdown.sh

This script uses a for loop to count down from 5 to 1, pausing 1 second between each number.

docs.title: backup script
docs.text:
#!/bin/zsh

DATE=$(date +"%Y-%m-%d-%H-%M")
cp -R ~/Documents ~/Documents_backup_$DATE

echo "Backup completed!"

docs.title: 🔐 Store Secrets Safely (API Keys)
docs.text: Never hardcode API keys—use env vars.

docs.title: Add to your ~/.zshrc:
docs.text:
export OPENAI_API_KEY="your-secret-key"
export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="another-secret-key"

Use in Python:

import os
api_key = os.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY")

Use in Node:

const apiKey = process.env.OPENAI_API_KEY;

docs.title: Quest 1: Make Your Own Command
docs.text:
Create:

motivate

Output:

You are unstoppable!

docs.title: Quest 2: PATH Detective
docs.text:
Run:

which python
which node
which git

docs.title: Quest 3: Daily Log
docs.text:
Append a timestamp:

echo "Logged at: $(date)" >> ~/daily_log.txt

docs.title: Quest 4: Secret Keeper
docs.text: Create an env var called SECRET_WORD and write a script that prints it.

docs.title: 🎓 End of Chapter 2
docs.text:
You now understand: - environment variables - export - PATH - .zshrc - creating .sh scripts - adding scripts to PATH - making custom commands

Next: 👉 Your First Web Server (Python & Node)

docs.title: 🗂️ Chapter 2.1 — Terminal, Finder, and VS Code: Three Views of the Same Files
docs.text: Level: Beginner Theme: Understanding how Mac Finder, Terminal, and VS Code all show the same files Tools: macOS Finder, Terminal, VS Code Goal: Build a mental model of how file systems work and how tools stay in sync.

docs.title: ⭐ Why This Lesson Matters
docs.text:
Beginners often think each tool shows a different set of files:

  • Terminal = “files”
  • Finder = “Mac files”
  • VS Code = “coding workspace”

But here’s the truth:

There is only ONE set of folders and files on your Mac. Terminal, Finder, and VS Code all display the same filesystem in different ways.

Understanding this is essential before working with Python, Node, GitHub, and larger projects.

docs.title: 🧰 Step 1 — Create a New Folder Using Terminal
docs.text:
cd ~
mkdir project-demo
cd project-demo

Check where you are:

pwd

List files:

ls

docs.title: 📁 Step 2 — Open the Same Folder in Finder
docs.text:
From Terminal:

open .

Finder opens the exact same folder: project-demo

docs.title: 📝 Step 3 — Open the Same Folder in VS Code
docs.text:
From Terminal:

code .

VS Code now displays the same folder in its sidebar.

docs.title: 🎉 At this point:
docs.text:
• Terminal
  • Finder
  • VS Code

Are all showing the same exact folder.

docs.title: ✏️ Step 4 — Create a File in Terminal
docs.text:
echo "Hello from the terminal!" > hello.txt

Check visibility across tools:

docs.title: Finder
docs.text: You’ll see hello.txt.

docs.title: VS Code
docs.text: It appears instantly in the file explorer.

docs.title: Terminal
docs.text:
ls
cat hello.txt

docs.title: 👨‍💻 Step 5 — Edit the File in VS Code and See It in Terminal
docs.text:
Open hello.txt → change the text → press Command + S.

Back in Terminal:

cat hello.txt

You will see the updated content instantly.

docs.title: 📁 Step 6 — Create a Subfolder in Finder
docs.text:
In Finder:

  • Right-click → New Folder
  • Name it: 
notes

Now check:

docs.title: VS Code
docs.text: notes/ appears immediately.

docs.title: Terminal
docs.text: ls

docs.title: 🖥️ Step 7 — Use the Built-in Terminal Inside VS Code
docs.text:
In VS Code:

  • Press Control + ` (control + backtick)
  • Or go to View → Terminal

Run:

pwd
ls

You will see the same files and folders.

docs.title: 🔁 Step 8 — Demonstrate Sync Across All Tools
From macOS Terminal:
docs.text: echo "from mac terminal" > mac.txt

docs.title: In VS Code Terminal:
docs.text:
ls
cat mac.txt

docs.title: In Finder
docs.text:
You’ll see mac.txt appear instantly.

One file system. Three different views.

docs.title: ✔ Exercise 1 — Create a folder in VS Code
docs.text: Right-click Explorer → New Folder Verify in Finder + Terminal.

docs.title: ✔ Exercise 2 — Create a file in Finder
docs.text: Verify in VS Code + Terminal.

docs.title: ✔ Exercise 3 — Create a file in VS Code
docs.text: Run ls and cat in Terminal to verify.

docs.title: ✔ Exercise 4 — Rename a file in Finder
docs.text: Verify rename shows up instantly in VS Code + Terminal.

docs.title: 🎓 End of Chapter 2.1
docs.text:
You now understand:

  • Terminal, Finder, and VS Code show the same real files
  • All tools update instantly when files or folders are created
  • Edits in VS Code appear immediately via cat in the Terminal
  • Subfolders created in Finder appear instantly everywhere
  • Both VS Code Terminal and macOS Terminal share the same filesystem

This prepares you for:

  • Chapter 3.1: Python programming
  • Chapter 3.2: HTML & JS
  • Chapter 6: Git & GitHub
  • All future development workflows

docs.title: 🗑️ Chapter 2.2 — Deleting Files and Folders Safely in Terminal
docs.text: Level: Beginner Theme: Learning safe file deletion Tools: macOS Terminal, Finder, VS Code Goal: Understand rm, rm -rf, and how to inspect files safely with ls -l.

docs.title: ⭐ Why File Deletion Requires Caution
docs.text:
Terminal deletions are instant and permanent. There is:

  • no Trash
  • no Undo
  • no confirmation (depending on the command)

This chapter teaches you how to delete safely and confidently.

docs.title: 🧰 Step 1 — Create a Practice Folder
docs.text:
cd ~
mkdir delete-practice
cd delete-practice

Create some files and a folder:

echo "hello" > file1.txt
echo "goodbye" > file2.txt
mkdir test-folder

List contents:

ls

docs.title: 🧭 Step 2 — Use ls -l to Inspect Files and Folders
docs.text:
ls -l

You will see something like:

-rw-r--r--  file1.txt
-rw-r--r--  file2.txt
drwxr-xr-x  test-folder

Meaning:

  • - = file
  • d = directory
  • last column = name
  • permissions + timestamps also shown

This is how you confirm exactly what you are about to delete.

docs.title: 🗑️ Step 3 — Delete a Single File with rm
docs.text:
rm file1.txt

Check:

ls

Delete the second file:

rm file2.txt

docs.title: 🗂️ Step 4 — Why rm Cannot Delete Folders
docs.text:
Try:

rm test-folder

Terminal will report:

rm: test-folder: is a directory

Because rm only deletes files.

docs.title: 📁 Step 5 — Delete a Folder with rm -r
docs.text:
The -r means recursive: delete the folder and everything inside it.

rm -r test-folder

Confirm:

ls

docs.title: ⚠️ Step 6 — Understanding the Danger of rm -rf
docs.text:
rm -rf means:

  • r → recursive
  • f → force (no warnings)
rm -rf foldername

⚠️ Extremely powerful. Use only when fully certain.

It will delete:

  • hidden files
  • nested folders
  • without asking
  • instantly
  • permanently

Never run these commands:

rm -rf /
rm -rf *

They can delete your entire system.

docs.title: ✔ Step 1 — Check your location
docs.text: pwd

docs.title: ✔ Step 2 — Review what’s inside
docs.text: ls -l

docs.title: ✔ Step 3 — Delete with intention
docs.text:
Use:

rm filename

or

rm -r foldername

Only use rm -rf when absolutely needed.

docs.title: ✔ Exercise 1 — Create and delete a folder
docs.text:
mkdir demo1
rm -r demo1

docs.title: ✔ Exercise 2 — Create multiple files and delete only one
docs.text:
echo "a" > a.txt
echo "b" > b.txt
ls -l
rm a.txt
ls -l

docs.title: ✔ Exercise 3 — Delete nested folders
docs.text:
mkdir -p level1/level2
echo "hello" > level1/level2/notes.txt
rm -r level1

docs.title: ✔ Exercise 4 — Observe deletion in VS Code + Finder
docs.text:
1. Create test.txt in VS Code
  2. Delete it using:
rm test.txt
  1. Watch it disappear in Finder and VS Code

docs.title: ✔ Exercise 5 — Mistake on purpose
docs.text:
Run:

rm -rf not-a-real-folder

You’ll see:

rm: not-a-real-folder: No such file or directory

This teaches safe error handling.

docs.title: 🎓 End of Chapter 2.2
docs.text:
You now understand:

  • how to inspect files using ls -l
  • how to delete files with rm
  • how to delete folders with rm -r
  • the power and danger of rm -rf
  • how to protect yourself from accidental deletion
  • how Finder, VS Code, and Terminal update instantly

docs.title: 🐍 Chapter 3.1 — Introduction to Python Programming on macOS
docs.text: Level: Beginner Theme: Your first real programming language Tools: macOS, Terminal, VS Code, Homebrew, Python 3.12 Goal: Learn the basics of Python, run scripts, use virtual environments, and prepare for more advanced AI + backend work.

docs.title: ⭐ Welcome to Python
docs.text:
Python is one of the easiest and most powerful languages in the world. It is used for:

  • AI and machine learning
  • web servers
  • automation
  • games
  • data analysis
  • robotics
  • scripting

In this chapter, you will:

  • install Python 3.12 using Homebrew
  • create a virtual environment
  • write your first Python script
  • run programs in VS Code
  • learn variables, input, loops, conditionals, and functions
  • build beginner-friendly mini projects

docs.title: 🧰 Step 1 — Install Python 3.12 (with Homebrew)
docs.text:
In Terminal:

brew install python@3.12

Check version:

python3 --version

You should see:

Python 3.12.x

docs.title: 📦 Step 2 — Create a Project Folder
docs.text:
mkdir python-basics
cd python-basics

docs.title: 🔒 Step 3 — Create Your Virtual Environment
docs.text:
python3 -m venv .venv

Activate:

source .venv/bin/activate

Deactivate when finished:

deactivate

docs.title: 📝 Step 4 — Open Project in VS Code
docs.text:
code .

If VS Code asks:

“Use this virtual environment?”

Click Yes.

docs.title: ✏️ Step 5 — Create Your First Python File
docs.text:
Create:

hello.py

Add:

print("Hello, young developer!")

Run:

python hello.py

docs.title: 1️⃣ Variables
docs.text:
name = "Alice"
age = 13
pi = 3.14

print(name, age, pi)

docs.title: 2️⃣ Input
docs.text:
your_name = input("What is your name? ")
print("Hello,", your_name)

docs.title: 3️⃣ Math
docs.text:
a = 10
b = 3

print(a + b)
print(a - b)
print(a * b)
print(a / b)
print(a ** b)

docs.title: 4️⃣ If Statements
docs.text:
age = int(input("How old are you? "))

if age >= 13:
    print("You can play this game!")
else:
    print("You are too young!")

docs.title: While:
docs.text:
count = 5
while count > 0:
    print("Counting:", count)
    count -= 1

docs.title: For:
docs.text:
for i in range(5):
    print("Loop number:", i)

docs.title: 6️⃣ Functions
docs.text:
def greet(name):
    print("Hello,", name)

greet("Alice")
greet("Bob")

docs.title: 🧮 Project 1 — Calculator
docs.text:
a = float(input("First number: "))
b = float(input("Second number: "))

print("Add:", a + b)
print("Subtract:", a - b)
print("Multiply:", a * b)

if b != 0:
    print("Divide:", a / b)
else:
    print("Cannot divide by zero!")

docs.title: 🎲 Project 2 — Dice Roller
docs.text:
import random

roll = random.randint(1, 6)
print("You rolled:", roll)

docs.title: 🔐 Project 3 — Password Checker
docs.text:
password = input("Enter password: ")

if password == "secret123":
    print("Access granted!")
else:
    print("Access denied!")

docs.title: 🕹️ Project 4 — Number Guessing Game
docs.text:
import random

secret = random.randint(1, 20)

guess = 0
while guess != secret:
    guess = int(input("Guess the number (1-20): "))

    if guess < secret:
        print("Too low!")
    elif guess > secret:
        print("Too high!")
    else:
        print("Correct!")

docs.title: 🎯 Next Steps
docs.text:
Now you're ready for:

  • Chapter 3: Building Python FastAPI servers
  • Chapter 4: SQLite databases
  • Chapter 5: Your first AI app
  • Chapter 6: Agents, tools, and iOS apps

docs.title: 🎓 End of Chapter 3.1
docs.text:
You now know how to:

  • install Python 3.12
  • use virtual environments
  • run Python scripts
  • use VS Code
  • understand variables, loops, input, functions
  • build simple interactive programs

docs.title: 🌐 Chapter 3 — Your First Web Server (Python + Node)
docs.text: Level: Beginner Theme: Making your computer talk to the internet Tools: MacBook Air, Terminal, VS Code Goal: Build your first web server and create real API endpoints.

docs.title: ⭐ Before We Begin: Install Python & Node with Homebrew
docs.text: We will use Homebrew, the package manager for macOS.

docs.title: Install Homebrew (skip if already installed):
docs.text: /bin/bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Homebrew/install/HEAD/install.sh)"

docs.title: 🐍 Install Python (via Homebrew)
docs.text:
First check to see if homebrew itself needs an update. Programs on a computer in constant need of update and many updates do not happen automatically as they do on a phone/tablet.

brew update

brew install python@3.12

Verify:

python3 --version
pip3 --version

docs.title: Create a virtual environment for Python projects:
docs.text:
mkdir my_project
cd my_project

Inside my_project folder:

python3 -m venv .venv

Activate it:

source .venv/bin/activate

Now, you no longer have to use python3

python -V

When you're done coding:

deactivate

docs.title: 🟦 Install Node.js (via Homebrew)
docs.text:
brew install node

Verify:

node -v
npm -v

docs.title: 🐍 PART 1 — Python FastAPI Web Server
docs.text: FastAPI is a modern, fast web framework for building APIs.

docs.title: 🧰 Step 1: Create Your Project Folder
docs.text:
mkdir python-server
cd python-server

docs.title: 🧪 Step 2: Create & Activate Virtual Environment
docs.text:
python3 -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate

docs.title: 📦 Step 3: Install FastAPI + Uvicorn
docs.text: pip install fastapi uvicorn

docs.title: 📝 Step 4: Create main.py
docs.text:
touch main.py
open -e main.py

Paste this:

from fastapi import FastAPI

app = FastAPI()

@app.get("/")
def read_root():
    return {"message": "Hello from your first Python server!"}

@app.get("/add")
def add(a: int, b: int):
    return {"result": a + b}

docs.title: 🚀 Step 5: Run Your Server
docs.text:
uvicorn main:app --reload

Visit: - http://127.0.0.1:8000 - http://127.0.0.1:8000/add?a=3&b=4

docs.title: 🟦 PART 2 — Node.js Express Web Server
docs.text: Express is the standard framework for building APIs in Node.

docs.title: 🧰 Step 1: Create a Project Folder
docs.text:
mkdir node-server
cd node-server

docs.title: 📦 Step 2: Initialize a Node Project
docs.text: npm init -y

docs.title: 📦 Step 3: Install Express
docs.text: npm install express

docs.title: 📝 Step 4: Create index.js
docs.text:
touch index.js
open -e index.js

Paste:

const express = require("express");
const app = express();

app.get("/", (req, res) => {
    res.json({ message: "Hello from your first Node server!" });
});

app.get("/add", (req, res) => {
    const a = Number(req.query.a);
    const b = Number(req.query.b);
    res.json({ result: a + b });
});

app.listen(3000, () => {
    console.log("Server running on http://127.0.0.1:3000");
});

docs.title: 🚀 Step 5: Run Your Server
docs.text:
node index.js

Open: - http://127.0.0.1:3000 - http://127.0.0.1:3000/add?a=5&b=7

docs.title: 🖥️ PART 3 — Build a Simple Web Page That Talks to Your Server
📝 Step 1: Create index.html
docs.text:
touch index.html
open -e index.html

Paste:

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
    <title>Calculator Client</title>
</head>
<body>
    <h1>Calculator</h1>

    <input id="num1" type="number" placeholder="Number 1" />
    <input id="num2" type="number" placeholder="Number 2" />
    <button onclick="calculate()">Add Them!</button>

    <h2 id="result"></h2>

    <script>
        async function calculate() {
            let n1 = document.getElementById("num1").value;
            let n2 = document.getElementById("num2").value;

            let response = await fetch(`http://127.0.0.1:8000/add?a=${n1}&b=${n2}`)
                .catch(() => alert("Server not running!"));

            let data = await response.json();

            document.getElementById("result").innerText =
                "Result: " + data.result;
        }
    </script>
</body>
</html>

docs.title: Quest 1 — Add More Math Endpoints
docs.text: Add /subtract, /multiply, /divide

docs.title: Quest 2 — Error Handling
docs.text: Prevent dividing by zero.

docs.title: 🎓 End of Chapter 3
docs.text: You now know how to: - Install Python & Node via Homebrew - Use Python virtual environments - Build a Python FastAPI server - Build a Node Express server - Return JSON responses - Build a webpage that calls your server

docs.title: 🌐 Chapter 3.2 — Introduction to HTML & JavaScript
docs.text: Level: Beginner Theme: Building your first web pages and connecting them to servers Tools: macOS, VS Code, Homebrew, Node.js, Python (FastAPI) Goal: Learn HTML + JS basics, install Node, and submit GET + POST requests to a server.

docs.title: ⭐ What You Will Learn
docs.text:
In this chapter you will:

  • install Node + npm
  • create HTML pages
  • write simple JavaScript
  • run a tiny web server in Node.js
  • run a tiny web server in Python (FastAPI)
  • make GET and POST requests
  • build a simple form that sends data to a backend

docs.title: 🧰 Step 1 — Install Node.js and npm via Homebrew
docs.text:
brew install node

Verify:

node -v
npm -v

docs.title: 📁 Step 2 — Create Your Web Project Folder
docs.text:
mkdir web-basics
cd web-basics

Open in VS Code:

code .

docs.title: 📝 Step 3 — Create Your First HTML Page (index.html)
docs.text:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
    <title>My First Web Page</title>
</head>
<body>
    <h1>Hello from HTML!</h1>

    <p id="message"></p>

    <button onclick="showMessage()">Click Me</button>

    <script src="script.js"></script>
</body>
</html>

docs.title: 🟨 Step 4 — Create Your First JavaScript File (script.js)
docs.text:
function showMessage() {
    document.getElementById("message").innerText =
        "You clicked the button! JavaScript is working!";
}

Open index.html in your browser to test.

docs.title: 🌍 Step 5 — Serving HTML + JS from a Node.js Web Server
docs.text:
Install Express:

npm init -y
npm install express

docs.title: 📝 Create server.js
docs.text:
const express = require("express");
const path = require("path");
const app = express();

app.use(express.static(__dirname));
app.use(express.json());

// GET endpoint
app.get("/hello", (req, res) => {
    res.json({ message: "Hello from Node.js server!" });
});

// POST endpoint
app.post("/submit", (req, res) => {
    res.json({ received: req.body });
});

app.listen(3000, () => {
    console.log("Server running at http://127.0.0.1:3000");
});

Run:

node server.js

docs.title: 📬 Step 6 — Updating JS to Use GET
docs.text:
Modify script.js:

async function showMessage() {
    let response = await fetch("/hello");
    let data = await response.json();

    document.getElementById("message").innerText = data.message;
}

docs.title: 📝 Step 7 — Add a Form That Uses POST
docs.text:
Update index.html:

<h2>Send a Message to the Server</h2>

<form onsubmit="sendForm(event)">
    <input id="textInput" placeholder="Type something…" />
    <button type="submit">Submit</button>
</form>

<p id="serverResponse"></p>

Update script.js:

async function sendForm(event) {
    event.preventDefault();

    let text = document.getElementById("textInput").value;

    let response = await fetch("/submit", {
        method: "POST",
        headers: { "Content-Type": "application/json" },
        body: JSON.stringify({ text })
    });

    let data = await response.json();
    document.getElementById("serverResponse").innerText =
        "Server received: " + data.received.text;
}

docs.title: 🐍 Step 8 — Serving the Same HTML + JS with Python (FastAPI)
docs.text:
Install:

pip install fastapi uvicorn

Create pyserver.py:

from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi.responses import FileResponse
from fastapi.middleware.cors import CORSMiddleware
from pydantic import BaseModel

app = FastAPI()

app.add_middleware(
    CORSMiddleware,
    allow_origins=["*"],
    allow_methods=["*"],
    allow_headers=["*"],
)

class FormData(BaseModel):
    text: str

@app.get("/")
def root():
    return FileResponse("index.html")

@app.get("/hello")
def hello():
    return {"message": "Hello from Python FastAPI!"}

@app.post("/submit")
def submit(data: FormData):
    return {"received": data.text}

Run:

uvicorn pyserver:app --reload

docs.title: 🗄️ Chapter 4 — Databases & SQLite: Teaching Your Apps to Remember
docs.text: Level: Beginner Theme: Saving information permanently Tools: MacBook Air, Terminal, VS Code Goal: Learn how databases work, create tables, insert data, read data, and build a backend that stores information.

docs.title: ⭐ Why Databases?
docs.text:
Imagine if your Python or Node server could remember things:

  • user accounts
  • high scores
  • notes
  • messages
  • todo lists

You need a database for that.

In this chapter, you’ll use SQLite, a small, fast, file-based database perfect for beginners.

SQLite databases are just single files like:

mydata.db

docs.title: 🧰 Step 1 — Install SQLite (via Homebrew)
docs.text:
brew install sqlite

Verify installation:

sqlite3 --version

docs.title: 🗃️ Step 2 — Create a Database File
docs.text:
Inside any project folder:

sqlite3 mydata.db

You will see the SQLite prompt:

sqlite>

To exit:

.exit

docs.title: 📋 Step 3 — Create a Table
docs.text:
Let's store simple notes.

Inside SQLite:

CREATE TABLE notes (
    id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
    content TEXT,
    created_at TEXT
);

Check the table:

.schema notes

docs.title: ✍️ Step 4 — Insert Data
docs.text:
INSERT INTO notes (content, created_at)
VALUES ("My first note!", datetime('now'));

docs.title: 📖 Step 5 — Read Data Back
docs.text: SELECT * FROM notes;

docs.title: 🧹 Step 6 — Delete & Update
docs.text:
Delete a note:

DELETE FROM notes WHERE id = 1;

Update a note:

UPDATE notes SET content = "Updated!" WHERE id = 2;

docs.title: 📦 Install SQLite support
docs.text: pip install aiosqlite

docs.title: 📝 Create a Notes API in FastAPI
docs.text:
from fastapi import FastAPI
import aiosqlite
from datetime import datetime

app = FastAPI()

DATABASE = "mydata.db"

@app.post("/add_note")
async def add_note(content: str):
    async with aiosqlite.connect(DATABASE) as db:
        await db.execute(
            "INSERT INTO notes (content, created_at) VALUES (?, ?)",
            (content, datetime.utcnow().isoformat())
        )
        await db.commit()
    return {"status": "ok", "message": "Note added."}

@app.get("/notes")
async def get_notes():
    async with aiosqlite.connect(DATABASE) as db:
        rows = await db.execute_fetchall("SELECT * FROM notes")
    return {"notes": rows}

Run it:

uvicorn main:app --reload

docs.title: 📦 Install SQLite library for Node
docs.text: npm install better-sqlite3

docs.title: 📝 Create a Notes API in Express
docs.text:
const express = require("express");
const Database = require("better-sqlite3");
const app = express();

const db = new Database("mydata.db");

app.get("/notes", (req, res) => {
    const rows = db.prepare("SELECT * FROM notes").all();
    res.json({ notes: rows });
});

app.post("/add_note", (req, res) => {
    const content = req.query.content;
    db.prepare(
        "INSERT INTO notes (content, created_at) VALUES (?, datetime('now'))"
    ).run(content);
    res.json({ status: "ok", message: "Note added." });
});

app.listen(3000, () => console.log("Running on http://127.0.0.1:3000"));

Run it:

node index.js

docs.title: 🌐 PART 3 — Basic Frontend for Notes App
docs.text:
Create notes.html:

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
    <title>My Notes App</title>
</head>
<body>

<h1>Notes App</h1>

<input id="text" placeholder="Write a note" />
<button onclick="addNote()">Add Note</button>

<ul id="notes"></ul>

<script>
async function loadNotes() {
    let res = await fetch("http://127.0.0.1:8000/notes");
    let data = await res.json();

    let list = document.getElementById("notes");
    list.innerHTML = "";

    data.notes.forEach(note => {
        let li = document.createElement("li");
        li.innerText = note[1];
        list.appendChild(li);
    });
}

async function addNote() {
    let text = document.getElementById("text").value;
    await fetch(`http://127.0.0.1:8000/add_note?content=${text}`, { method: "POST" });
    loadNotes();
}

loadNotes();
</script>

</body>
</html>

docs.title: 🤖 Chapter 5 — Your First AI App: Teaching Computers to Think
docs.text: Level: Beginner/Intermediate Theme: Adding intelligence to your applications Tools: Python or Node.js, API Keys, JSON Goal: Build your first AI-powered app that sends prompts to an AI model and receives smart responses.

docs.title: ⭐ What You Are About to Build
docs.text:
In this chapter, you will:

  • get an AI API key
  • call an AI model using Python or Node
  • build a tiny AI chatbot
  • build a simple AI-powered web page
  • learn prompts, messages, responses, and tokens
  • understand what happens “behind the curtain”

By the end, you'll have your own AI assistant, just like a mini ChatGPT.

docs.text: https://console.anthropic.com/

docs.text: https://console.groq.com/

docs.text: via Ollama, Llama-Stack, etc.

docs.title: 🔐 Step 2 — Store Your API Key in an Environment Variable
docs.text:
Add to your ~/.zshrc:

export OPENAI_API_KEY="your-secret-key-here"

Reload:

source ~/.zshrc

docs.title: 🧰 Step 1 — Project Setup
docs.text:
mkdir ai-chatbot
cd ai-chatbot
python3 -m vvenv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate
pip install openai

docs.title: 📝 Step 2 — Create chat.py
docs.text:
from openai import OpenAI
import os

client = OpenAI(api_key=os.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY"))

while True:
    user_input = input("You: ")

    if user_input.lower() in ["exit", "quit"]:
        break

    response = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o-mini",
        messages=[
            {"role": "user", "content": user_input}
        ]
    )

    print("AI:", response.choices[0].message["content"])

Run:

python chat.py

docs.title: 🧰 Step 1 — Setup
docs.text:
mkdir ai-chatbot-node
cd ai-chatbot-node
npm init -y
npm install openai

docs.title: 📝 Step 2 — Create chat.js
docs.text:
import OpenAI from "openai";
import readline from "readline";

const client = new OpenAI({
    apiKey: process.env.OPENAI_API_KEY
});

const rl = readline.createInterface({
    input: process.stdin,
    output: process.stdout
});

function ask() {
    rl.question("You: ", async (input) => {
        if (input === "exit") return rl.close();

        const response = await client.chat.completions.create({
            model: "gpt-4o-mini",
            messages: [{ role: "user", content: input }]
        });

        console.log("AI:", response.choices[0].message.content);
        ask();
    });
}

ask();

Run:

node chat.js

docs.title: 🌐 PART 3 — Build a Simple AI Web App
🧰 Backend (Python FastAPI)
docs.text:
Install:

pip install fastapi uvicorn openai

Create server.py:

from fastapi import FastAPI
from pydantic import BaseModel
from openai import OpenAI
import os

client = OpenAI(api_key=os.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY"))
app = FastAPI()

class Query(BaseModel):
    message: str

@app.post("/chat")
def chat(query: Query):
    resp = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o-mini",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": query.message}]
    )
    return {"reply": resp.choices[0].message["content"]}

Run:

uvicorn server:app --reload

docs.title: 🖥️ Frontend (index.html)
docs.text:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
    <title>AI Chat</title>
</head>
<body>

<h1>AI Chatbot</h1>

<input id="msg" placeholder="Say something..." />
<button onclick="send()">Send</button>

<p id="reply"></p>

<script>
async function send() {
    let text = document.getElementById("msg").value;

    let res = await fetch("http://127.0.0.1:8000/chat", {
        method: "POST",
        headers: { "Content-Type": "application/json" },
        body: JSON.stringify({ message: text })
    });

    let data = await res.json();
    document.getElementById("reply").innerText = data.reply;
}
</script>

</body>
</html>

Open in your browser and start chatting.

docs.title: 🗂️ Chapter 6 — Introduction to Git & GitHub
docs.text: Level: Beginner Theme: Learning version control with Git Tools: macOS, Terminal, VS Code, Homebrew, Git Goal: Install Git, create your first GitHub repo, clone it locally, make changes, commit, and push.

docs.title: Git
docs.text: A tool on your computer that tracks changes to files over time — like a save-game system for coding.

docs.title: GitHub
docs.text: A website where your Git repositories live online. Think of it like iCloud for your code, but more powerful.

docs.title: 🧰 Step 1 — Install Git Using Homebrew
docs.text:
brew install git

Verify:

git --version

docs.title: 👤 Step 2 — Sign Up for a GitHub Account
docs.text:

  2. Click Sign Up
  3. Create username, email, and password
  4. Verify email

docs.title: 📦 Step 3 — Create Your First GitHub Repository
docs.text:
1. Log in
  2. Click New
  3. Name it:
my-first-project
  1. Choose Public
  2. Click Create repository

Your repo is created — but empty.

docs.title: 🖥️ Step 4 — Clone the Repository to Your Mac

cd ~

code .

docs.title: 🌐 Chapter 7 — Introduction to Networking Tools
docs.text: Level: Beginner Theme: How computers communicate across the internet Tools: macOS Terminal Goal: Learn basic networking tools: ping, curl, wget, traceroute, nslookup, dig.

docs.title: ⭐ Why Networking Tools Matter
docs.text:
When you build apps—websites, APIs, servers, games—they must talk to other computers. Networking tools help you:

  • test connectivity
  • measure latency
  • download files
  • send HTTP requests
  • debug networking issues
  • see how the internet routes your traffic

These skills make you a real developer.

docs.title: 🧰 Step 1 — Using ping
docs.text:
Test if a server is reachable:

ping google.com

Stop it with:

Control + C

Ping your own machine:

ping 127.0.0.1

docs.title: 🌍 Step 2 — Using curl
docs.text:
Send an HTTP GET request:

curl https://google.com

Talk to your local Node server:

curl http://127.0.0.1:3000/hello

Send POST JSON:

curl -X POST -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
     -d '{"text":"hello"}' http://127.0.0.1:8000/submit

docs.title: 📥 Step 3 — Using wget (install via Homebrew)
docs.text:
Install:

brew install wget

Download a file:

wget https://example.com/file.txt

Download your own page:

wget http://127.0.0.1:3000/index.html

docs.title: 🛰️ Step 4 — Using traceroute
docs.text:
Shows every hop between your computer and a server.

Install:

brew install traceroute

Run:

traceroute google.com

docs.title: 🌐 Step 5 — Using nslookup (DNS lookup)
docs.text:
nslookup google.com

See DNS servers + IP addresses.

docs.title: 🔍 Step 6 — Using dig
docs.text:
Install:

brew install bind

Basic query:

dig google.com

Short answer:

dig google.com +short

Mail records:

dig google.com MX

docs.title: 🗺️ Networking Map
docs.text:
[Your Computer]
      ↓  curl / browser
[DNS Lookup — dig/nslookup]
      ↓
[IP Address]
      ↓
[Internet Hops — traceroute]
